Quantum Kettle is piloting an instant-delivery pass in three downtown neighborhoods. The market research team estimates the following daily demand and supply curves.

Demand is Qd = 128 - 3P and supply is Qs = 13 + 4P. A per-unit seller tax of $5 is under consideration.

(b) With a seller tax, Qs = 13 + 4(Pc - 5). Set 128 - 3Pc = 13 + 4(Pc - 5). Thus Pc = $19.29, sellers receive Ps = $14.29, and Q = 70.14.

problem_text:
Aurora Scooters is testing whether a premium product can support a higher price.

Aurora Scooters raises the price of its premium snack box from $22 to $26. Weekly sales fall from 480 boxes to 415 boxes.
solution:
(a) Percentage change in quantity is (415 - 480)/480 = -13.54%. Percentage change in price is (26 - 22)/22 = 18.18%. Elasticity is -0.74.

(b) Demand is elastic if the absolute value exceeds 1 and inelastic if it is below 1.

(c) Initial revenue is $10560. New revenue is $10790. Revenue increases after the price increase.

problem_text:
The city council is considering an intervention in a permit market involving Flux Fitness.

Demand is Qd = 95 - 2P and supply is Qs = 7 + 2P. The proposed price floor is $31.50.
solution:
(a) Set demand equal to supply: 95 - 2P = 7 + 2P. So P = $22 and Q = 51.

(b) The floor is above the equilibrium price of $22, so it is binding.

(c) At $31.50, Qd = 32 and Qs = 70. There is excess supply of 38 units.

The city council is considering an intervention in a permit market involving Prism Produce.

Demand is Qd = 145 - 2P and supply is Qs = 17 + 2P. The proposed price floor is $46.50.

(b) The floor is above the equilibrium price of $32, so it is binding.

(c) At $46.50, Qd = 52 and Qs = 110. There is excess supply of 58 units.

problem_text:
Polaris Bikes is deciding how many units to produce during a short-run pilot.

The firm's short-run cost is C(q) = 90 + 7q + q^2. The current market price is $27.
solution:
(a) Marginal cost is MC = 7 + 2q. For positive output in competition, set P = MC, so q = (P - 7)/2 when P is at least 7.

(b) q = (27 - 7)/2 = 10.

(c) Profit is Pq - C(q) = $10. The shutdown price is minimum AVC, which is $7 here because AVC = 7 + q.

problem_text:
Nova Basket is designing prices for two customer segments.

High-value customers are willing to pay $73; low-value customers are willing to pay $40. Marginal cost is $11, and there is one customer of each type.
solution:
(a) Charge each type its willingness to pay: $73 to high-value customers and $40 to low-value customers. Profit is $91.

(b) At $40, both buy and profit is 2 x ($40 - $11) = $58. At $73, only high-value customers buy and profit is $62. The better single price is $73.

(c) Perfect price discrimination extracts each customer's surplus. A single price leaves some surplus or excludes one segment.

problem_text:
The pricing team at Prism Produce can sometimes identify buyer types.

High-value customers are willing to pay $79; low-value customers are willing to pay $44. Marginal cost is $13, and there is one customer of each type.
solution:
(a) Charge each type its willingness to pay: $79 to high-value customers and $44 to low-value customers. Profit is $97.

(b) At $44, both buy and profit is 2 x ($44 - $13) = $62. At $79, only high-value customers buy and profit is $66. The better single price is $79.

(c) Perfect price discrimination extracts each customer's surplus. A single price leaves some surplus or excludes one segment.

problem_text:
A buyer is considering a risky product made by Prism Produce.

The product is worth $1100 if it works and $0 if it fails. The failure probability is 6%.
solution:
(a) For a risk-neutral buyer, willingness to pay equals expected value: (1 - 0.06) x $1100 = $1034.

(b) Producer surplus is price minus cost: $1034 - $954 = $80.

(c) Risk aversion lowers willingness to pay below expected value when the product creates uninsured downside risk.

problem_text:
Buyers cannot observe quality before purchasing from sellers like Atlas Droneworks.

A reliable unit is worth $105 to consumers and costs $72 to make. An unreliable unit is worth $44 and costs $26. Initially, half the units are reliable.
solution:
(a) The pooled willingness to pay is 0.5 x $105 + 0.5 x $44 = $74.50.

(b) Reliable sellers need at least $72; unreliable sellers need $26. At $74.50, both types sell.

(c) If the pooled price is too low for reliable sellers, high-quality products exit and average quality falls.

problem_text:
Quantum Kettle and a rival must choose prices before seeing each other's decisions.

Quantum Kettle and Halo Health simultaneously choose High Price or Low Price. Payoffs are profits in thousands. If both choose High, payoffs are (52, 50). If Quantum Kettle chooses Low while Halo Health chooses High, payoffs are (60, 42). If Quantum Kettle chooses High while Halo Health chooses Low, payoffs are (43, 59). If both choose Low, payoffs are (45, 44).
solution:
(a) If Halo Health chooses High, Quantum Kettle prefers Low because 60 > 52. If Halo Health chooses Low, Quantum Kettle prefers Low because 45 > 43. Low Price is dominant.

(b) By the same comparison, Halo Health prefers Low whether Quantum Kettle chooses High or Low. Low Price is dominant.

(c) The Nash equilibrium is both firms choosing Low Price, since each is playing its dominant strategy.

problem_text:
Vertex Coffee and one rival compete by choosing quantities.

Market inverse demand is P = 95 - Q, where Q is total output. Both firms have constant marginal cost $19.
solution:
(a) Firm i maximizes (95 - qi - qj - 19)qi. The FOC gives 95 - 19 - 2qi - qj = 0, so qi = (76 - qj)/2.

(b) In symmetry qi = qj = q, so q = (76 - q)/2. Thus 3q = 76, q = 25.33, and price is $44.33.

(c) Each firm earns (P - MC)q = ($44.33 - $19) x 25.33 = $641.78.

Sapphire Solar faces no charge for the damage created by each unit it sells.

Inverse demand is P = 140 - Q, private marginal cost is $35, and external marginal damage is $23 per unit.
solution:
(a) Competition sets P = private MC: 140 - Q = 35, so Q = 105.

(b) Social marginal cost is $58. Set 140 - Q = 58, so Q = 82.

(c) A Pigouvian tax equal to marginal damage, $23, makes firms internalize the external cost.
